turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has our opinion in case 16-1144, Marinello versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: There is a statutory clause which is called the Omnibus Clause in the Internal Revenue Code and that clause makes it a felony "corruptly or by force to "endeavor to obstruct or impede the due administration of this title," i.e. the tax code. The question here is how broadly we should interpret this language? Does it cover efforts to impede virtually any and every tax related administrative activity in the Internal Revenue Code or is it more limited in scope, covering only specific administrative proceedings such as a pending investigation or audit? Well, we conclude that to show a violation of the clause the government has to show a nexus between the defendant's conduct in a particular administrative proceeding such as an investigation or an audit or the like, and that proceeding must have been pending or at least reasonably foreseeable at the time the defendant engaged in the obstructive conduct, that is a narrow interpretation. In interpreting the Omnibus Clause we follow the approach this Court set forth in United States v. Aguilar, a case in which we interpreted the statute that made it a felony, very similar language, corruptly or by threats of force to impede the due administration of justice. There we held that the statute contained a similar nexus requirement. We said that the obstructive conduct had to have a nexus to a particular judicial proceeding. We adopted this more targeted interpretation of the statute because we "have traditionally exercised restraint in assessing the reach of a federal criminal statute out of deference to the prerogatives of Congress and out of concern that a fair warning should be given to the world in language that the common world will understand, of what the law intends to do if a certain line is passed." We believe those same considerations apply here. Congress embedded the Omnibus Clause in the middle of a statutory paragraph that focuses more narrowly upon obstructing government officers or employees, not impeding any and every part of what the government has called these, this is are the government's word, "continuous, ubiquitous, and universally known" tax code. Moreover, to read the statute broadly risks transforming quite a few activities that the code classifies as misdemeanors into felonies. The government points out correctly that only corrupt impeding is at issue. It contends that the statutory word corruptly will adequately limit the statute's scope to only those who impede in order to obtain an unlawful tax benefit for themselves or for others. But in our view the word corruptly does not perform the necessary limiting job. Imagine, for example, that you are a passenger in a taxi or a customer in a restaurant and the driver or the waiter asks you to pay the fare or leave the tip in cash. If so, might you not suspect that the driver or waiter does not intend to report the cash as income? A broad reading of the clause could expose the passenger or diner who leaves cash to the risk of prosecution for a federal felony, so could paying a babysitter $41 per week in cash for a year without withholding taxes. And these are but a few of potentially many examples of a broad interpretation, transforming into felonies, conduct that "the common world" might believe was not a felony. In our view Congress would have spoken more clearly had it intended to bring about this kind of result. The government suggests that it would never prosecute such cases, restaurant customers, taxi passengers, those who employ babysitters and the like, and we assume that is so, but we have not construed and we have said previously that we cannot construe highly abstract, generally phrased criminal statutes simply on the assumption that the government will use the resulting broad prosecutorial authority responsibly. A broad interpretation, placing great power in the hands of the prosecution, risks non-uniformity in the statute's execution across time, in different geographical locations, and in so far as the public fears arbitrary prosecution, it risks undermining necessary public confidence in the criminal justice system. That is why, as we said in Aguilar, we have "traditionally exercised restraint in assessing the reach of a federal criminal statute." We do so now. Because the jury instructions in this case allowed the jury to find the defendant guilty without finding that he was aware of a particular pending or foreseeable proceeding, we vacate the decision of the Court of Appeals and return the case to that court for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Alito has joined.
